segments.text: Chapter 24 Lipid Biosynthesis

24.1 – How Are Fatty Acids Synthesized?
Fatty Acid Biosynthesis and Degradation Pathways are Different
As in cases of glycolysis/gluconeogenesis and glycogen synthesis/breakdown, fatty acid synthesis and degradation go by different routes
There are four major differences between fatty acid breakdown and biosynthesis

The Differences Between Fatty Acid Biosynthesis and Fatty Acid Breakdown
Intermediates in synthesis are linked to -SH groups of acyl carrier proteins (ACP) as compared to -SH groups of CoA
Synthesis in cytosol ; breakdown in mitochondria
Enzymes of synthesis are one polypeptide, the fatty acid synthase (FAS)
Biosynthesis uses NADPH  NADP + ; breakdown uses NAD +  NADH

FA Biosyntheis
Mitochondria
Cytoplasm
Acetyl-CoA
Citrate
Shuttle
Acetyl-CoA
1
Malonyl CoA
2
FA
1
Acetyl CoA carboxylase (ACC):
Adds CO 2 to Acetyl CoA to make malonyl CoA
Requires Biotin
Activated by Insulin , Inhibited by glucagon, epinephrine
2
FA synthase: adds 2 carbons at a time to make FA chain
Needs NADPH

Formation of Malonyl-CoA Activates Acetate Units for Fatty Acid Synthesis
The design strategy for fatty acid synthesis:
First acetyl - CoA is transported to the cytoplasm through the citrate shuttle
the acetate (2C) units are activated by addition of CO2 to form malonyl -CoA (3C) by acetyl CoA carboxylase (ACC)
The addition to the growing chain is attached to acyl-carrier protein (ACP)
The elongation reactions: addition of 2 carbons (3 from Malonyl , -1 from CO2) until the growing chain reaches 16 carbons in length ( palmitic acid )
Other enzymes then add double bonds and additional carbon units to the chain (if needed)

First Step: citrate shuttle
When ATP levels are high, Citrate accumulates in the mitocondria
Citrate crosses into the cytoplasm though the citrate shuttle
Citrate is converted back to Acetyl-CoA by Citrate Lyase
Acetyl-CoA is converted to Malonyl -CoA by ACC

Formation of Malonyl-CoA Activates Acetate Units for Fatty Acid Synthesis
The design strategy for fatty acid synthesis:
First acetyl - CoA is transported to the cytoplasm through the citrate shuttle
the acetate (2C) units are activated by addition of CO2 to form malonyl -CoA (3C) by acetyl CoA carboxylase (ACC)
The addition to the growing chain is attached to acyl-carrier protein (ACP)
The elongation reactions: addition of 2 carbons (3 from Malonyl , -1 from CO2) until the growing chain reaches 16 carbons in length ( palmitic acid )
Other enzymes then add double bonds and additional carbon units to the chain (if needed)

Carboxylation of Acetyl-CoA to Form Malonyl-CoA is Catalyzed by Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase
The "ACC enzyme" commits acetate to fatty 		acid synthesis
Carboxylation of acetyl-CoA to form malonyl -CoA is the irreversible, committed step in fatty acid biosynthesis
ACC uses bicarbonate and ATP (AND biotin )
E.coli enzyme has three subunits
Animal enzyme is one polypeptide with all three functions:
biotin carboxyl carrier protein
biotin carboxylase
carboxyltransferase

The Structure of the Biotin Carboxylase Domain of Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase
The biotin carboxylase domain from human acetyl-CoA carboxylase 2, with the A-subdomain in blue, the B-subdomain in red, the A-B linker in green, and the C-subdomain in yellow.

Acetate Units Are Committed to Fatty Acid Synthesis by Formation of Malonyl-CoA
Figure 24.2a The acetyl-CoA carboxylase reaction produces malonyl-CoA for fatty acid synthesis.

Acetate Units Are Committed to Fatty Acid Synthesis by Formation of Malonyl-CoA
Figure 24.2b A mechanism for the acetyl-CoA carboxylase reaction.  Bicarbonate is activated for carboxylation reactions by formation of N-carboxybiotin.  ATP drives the reaction forward, with transient formation of a carbonylphosphate intermediate.

Acetate Units Are Committed to Fatty Acid Synthesis by Formation of Malonyl-CoA
Figure 24.2 In a typical biotin-dependent reaction, nucleophilic attack by the acetyl-CoA carbanion on the carboxyl carbon of N-carboxybiotin – a transcarboxylation – yields the carboxylated product.

Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase in E. coli Consists of Three Subunits
Figure 24.3 In the ACC reaction, the biotin ring acquires carboxyl groups from carbonyl-phosphate on the biotin carboxylase subunit.

Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase in E. coli Consists of Three Subunits
Figure 24.3 Biotin on a flexible tether delivers carboxyl groups from the carboxylase to the carboxyltransferase .

Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase in Animals is a Multifunctional Protein
Active ACC forms a long filamentous polymer from inactive protomers
Protein polymerization = activation
As a committed step, ACC is carefully regulated
Palmitoyl -CoA (product) favors monomers
Citrate favors the active polymeric form
Phosphorylation modulates citrate activation and palmitoyl -CoA inhibition

ACC Phosphorylation Modulates Activation by Citrate and Inhibition by Palmitoyl-CoA
Unphosphorylation makes ACC more prone to activation
P hophorylation makes ACC more prone to inhibition
Unphosphorylated E has
high affinity for citrate = active at low [citrate ]
high K i for palmitoyl -CoA = inhibited at high [ palmitoyl - CoA]
Phosphorylated E has:
low affinity for citrate = high [citrate] is needed to activate
low K i for palmitoyl -CoA = is inhibited at low [ palmitoyl -CoA ]

Formation of Malonyl-CoA Activates Acetate Units for Fatty Acid Synthesis
The design strategy for fatty acid synthesis:
First acetyl - CoA is transported to the cytoplasm through the citrate shuttle
the acetate (2C) units are activated by addition of CO2 to form malonyl -CoA (3C) by acetyl CoA carboxylase (ACC)
T he growing FA chain is attached to acyl-carrier protein (ACP)
The elongation reactions: addition of 2 carbons (3 from Malonyl , -1 from CO2) until the growing chain reaches 16 carbons in length ( palmitic acid )
Other enzymes then add double bonds and additional carbon units to the chain (if needed)

Acyl Carrier Proteins Carry the Intermediates in Fatty Acid Synthesis
Acyl carrier proteins are the carriers  of intermediates in fatty acid synthesis
Discovered by P. Roy Vagelos - a 77-residue protein in E. coli - with a phosphopantetheine
In terms of function, it ’ s a large “ CoA ”

Acyl Carrier Proteins Carry the Intermediates in Fatty Acid Synthesis
Figure 24.6 Fatty acids are conjugated both to coenzyme A and to acyl carrier protein through the sulfhydryl group of phosphopantetheine prosthetic groups .
Acyl-group = acetyl or malonyl (attached to thiol )

FA synthesis
MalonylCoA (3C)
AcetylCoA (2C)
(a) & (b) = transfer of acyl-CoA to Acyl Carrier proteins
(c) = Combining malonyl (3C) + acetyl (2) – CO 2 (1C) = 4 C
(d – f) = reduction of ketone to alkane using NADPH
Process is repeated, adding 2 at a time by
adding Malonyl (3C) and losing CO2 (1C)
Lost as CO 2

Fatty Acid Synthesis
in bacteria catalyzed by 6 different enzymes plus a separate acyl carrier protein (ACP)
in mammals catalyzed by individual domains of a very large polypeptide that includes an ACP domain.
Evolution of the mammalian Fatty Acid Synthase apparently has involved gene fusion .
The condensation reaction (step 3) involves decarboxylation of the malonyl moiety, followed by attack of the resultant carbanion on the carbonyl carbon of the acetyl (or acyl) moiety .

The b -ketone is reduced to an alcohol by e - transfer from NADPH.
Dehydration yields a trans double bond.
Reduction by NADPH yields a saturated chain.
Following transfer of the growing fatty acid from phosphopantetheine to the Condensing Enzyme's cysteine sulfhydryl, the cycle begins again , with another malonyl-CoA.

Product release:
When the fatty acid is 16 carbon atoms long, a Thioesterase domain catalyzes hydrolysis of the thioester linking the fatty acid to phosphopantetheine .
The 16-C saturated fatty acid palmitate is the final product of the Fatty Acid Synthase complex.

Structure of the Fungal Fatty Acid Synthase
Figure 24.9  Fungal FAS is a closed barrel.  The arrangement of the functional domains along the FAS α and β polypeptides is shown at the bottom.

Structure of Mammalian Fatty Acid Synthase
Figure 24.9  Mammalian FAS is an asymmetric X-shape.  The arrangement of the functional domains along the FAS polypeptide is shown at the bottom.

Regulation of Fatty Acid Synthesis
Regulatory control of fatty acid metabolism is an interplay of allosteric modifiers, phosphorylation and dephosphorylation cycles and hormones
Malonyl-CoA blocks the carnitine acyltransferase and thus inhibits beta-oxidation
Citrate activates acetyl-CoA carboxylase
Fatty acyl-CoAs inhibit acetyl-CoA carboxylase
Hormones regulate acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC)
Glucagon activates lipases/inhibits ACC
Insulin inhibits lipases/activates ACC

Regulation of Fatty Acid Synthesis
Figure 24.16 Regulation of fatty acid synthesis and fatty acid oxidation are coupled as shown.
